# Tables and Figures

\* Significance levels: \* p\<0.10, \*\* p\<0.05, \*\*\*p\<0.01. † Indicate insignificant (p\<0.05) variation across resource extraction status.

^a^ The trend was estimated via a linear regression for continuous variables and a logit model for dummies.

The mean mid-rate interbank FX rate between the Ghana cedi (GH₵) and the US Dollar (\$) for December 2019 was 5.54 GH₵/\$ as reported by the Bank of Ghana

Data Sources: Ghana Living Standards Survey \[waves 4-7\]

#### Table 2. The prevalence of natural resource extraction in Ghanaian crop-producing communities

Data Sources: Ghana Living Standards Survey \[waves 4-7\]

#### Table 3. Natural resource extraction impact on input elasticities and variability in crop production metrics in Ghana 

+--------------------------------------------+---------------------+-------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+---+---------------------+---------------------+
|                                            | **Naïve national\   | ** ** | **Group frontier**  | ** **               | **Meta-frontier**    |   |                     |                     |
|                                            | frontier**          |       |                     |                     |                      |   |                     |                     |
+============================================+=====================+=======+=====================+=====================+======================+===+=====================+=====================+
|                                            |                     |       | **No extraction**   | **Some extraction** | **Difference**       |   | **Matched**         | **Unmatched**       |
|                                            |                     |       |                     |                     |                      |   |                     |                     |
|                                            |                     |       | **\[A\]**           | **\[B\]**           | **\[B-A\]**          |   |                     |                     |
+--------------------------------------------+---------------------+-------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+---+---------------------+---------------------+
| **[Elasticity]{.underline}**               |                     |       |                     |                     |                      |   |                     |                     |
+--------------------------------------------+---------------------+-------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+---+---------------------+---------------------+
| > Land                                     | 0.706\*\*\* (0.002) |       | 0.706\*\*\* (0.002) | 0.760\*\*\* (0.020) | 0.053\*\* (0.020)    |   | 0.696\*\*\* (0.004) | 0.703\*\*\* (0.005) |
+--------------------------------------------+---------------------+-------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+---+---------------------+---------------------+
| > Planting material                        | 0.048\*\*\* (0.000) |       | 0.051\*\*\* (0.000) | 0.023\*\*\* (0.001) | -0.023\*\*\* (0.001) |   | 0.040\*\*\* (0.001) | 0.048\*\*\* (0.001) |
+--------------------------------------------+---------------------+-------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+---+---------------------+---------------------+
| > Family labor                             | 0.206\*\*\* (0.001) |       | 0.199\*\*\* (0.001) | 0.255\*\*\* (0.002) | 0.054\*\*\* (0.003)  |   | 0.206\*\*\* (0.004) | 0.203\*\*\* (0.002) |
+--------------------------------------------+---------------------+-------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+---+---------------------+---------------------+
| > Hired labor                              | 0.023\*\*\* (0.000) |       | 0.023\*\*\* (0.000) | 0.032\*\*\* (0.001) | 0.009\*\*\* (0.001)  |   | 0.027\*\*\* (0.001) | 0.025\*\*\* (0.001) |
+--------------------------------------------+---------------------+-------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+---+---------------------+---------------------+
| > Fertilizer                               | 0.019\*\*\* (0.000) |       | 0.020\*\*\* (0.000) | 0.023\*\*\* (0.000) | 0.002\*\*\* (0.000)  |   | 0.026\*\*\* (0.001) | 0.020\*\*\* (0.001) |
+--------------------------------------------+---------------------+-------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+---+---------------------+---------------------+
| > Pesticide                                | 0.010\*\*\* (0.000) |       | 0.010\*\*\* (0.000) | 0.010\*\*\* (0.001) | 0.000 (0.001)        |   | 0.012\*\*\* (0.001) | 0.010\*\*\* (0.000) |
+--------------------------------------------+---------------------+-------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+---+---------------------+---------------------+
| > Returns to scale                         | 1.012\*\*\* (0.002) |       | 1.010\*\*\* (0.002) | 1.103\*\*\* (0.020) | 0.095\*\*\* (0.021)  |   | 1.007\*\*\* (0.005) | 1.009\*\*\* (0.006) |
+--------------------------------------------+---------------------+-------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+---+---------------------+---------------------+
| **[Technology/efficiency]{.underline}**    |                     |       |                     |                     |                      |   |                     |                     |
+--------------------------------------------+---------------------+-------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+---+---------------------+---------------------+
| > Technology gap ratio (TGR)               |                     |       |                     |                     |                      |   |                     |                     |
+--------------------------------------------+---------------------+-------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+---+---------------------+---------------------+
| > Matched                                  | \-                  |       | 0.819\*\*\* (0.011) | 0.726\*\*\* (0.019) | -0.093\*\*\* (0.009) |   | \-                  | \-                  |
+--------------------------------------------+---------------------+-------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+---+---------------------+---------------------+
| > Unmatched                                | \-                  |       | 0.924\*\*\* (0.008) | 0.752\*\*\* (0.007) | -0.171\*\*\* (0.005) |   | \-                  | \-                  |
+--------------------------------------------+---------------------+-------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+---+---------------------+---------------------+
| > Pure farmer technical efficiency (TE)    |                     |       |                     |                     |                      |   |                     |                     |
+--------------------------------------------+---------------------+-------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+---+---------------------+---------------------+
| > Matched                                  | 0.445\*\*\* (0.001) |       | 0.454\*\*\* (0.001) | 0.511\*\*\* (0.003) | 0.057\*\*\* (0.003)  |   | \-                  | \-                  |
+--------------------------------------------+---------------------+-------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+---+---------------------+---------------------+
| > Unmatched                                | 0.499\*\*\* (0.001) |       | 0.507\*\*\* (0.001) | 0.509\*\*\* (0.003) | 0.002 (0.003)        |   | \-                  | \-                  |
+--------------------------------------------+---------------------+-------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+---+---------------------+---------------------+
| > Meta-frontier technical efficiency (MTE) |                     |       |                     |                     |                      |   |                     |                     |
+--------------------------------------------+---------------------+-------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+---+---------------------+---------------------+
| > Matched                                  | 0.358\*\*\* (0.008) |       | 0.371\*\*\* (0.005) | 0.349\*\*\* (0.010) | -0.022\*\*\* (0.006) |   | \-                  | \-                  |
+--------------------------------------------+---------------------+-------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+---+---------------------+---------------------+
| > Unmatched                                | 0.460\*\*\* (0.004) |       | 0.473\*\*\* (0.004) | 0.363\*\*\* (0.003) | -0.110\*\*\* (0.002) |   | \-                  | \-                  |
+--------------------------------------------+---------------------+-------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+---+---------------------+---------------------+
| **[Model diagnostics]{.underline}**        |                     |       |                     |                     |                      |   |                     |                     |
+--------------------------------------------+---------------------+-------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+---+---------------------+---------------------+
| > Sample size                              | 26811               |       | 23957               | 2854                | \-                   |   | 5572                | 26811               |
+--------------------------------------------+---------------------+-------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+---+---------------------+---------------------+
| > Monotonicity satisfaction rate           | 53.35               |       | 73.47               | 76.04               | \-                   |   | 89.99               | 99.88               |
+--------------------------------------------+---------------------+-------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+---+---------------------+---------------------+
| > Curvature satisfaction rate              | 13.83               |       | 26.78               | 29.34               | \-                   |   | 71.44               | 41.06               |
+--------------------------------------------+---------------------+-------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+---+---------------------+---------------------+
| > Schmidt & Lin (1984) ^a^                 | -0.258\*\*\*        |       | -0.247\*\*\*        | -0.205\*\*\*        | \-                   |   | -0.702\*\*\*        | -3.245\*\*\*        |
+--------------------------------------------+---------------------+-------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+---+---------------------+---------------------+
| > Coelli, (1995) ^a^                       | -8.342\*\*\*        |       | -7.568\*\*\*        | -2.173              | \-                   |   | -12.308\*\*\*       | -114.307\*\*\*      |
+--------------------------------------------+---------------------+-------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+---+---------------------+---------------------+
| > Gutierrez (2001) ^a^                     | 592.590\*\*         |       | 543.844\*\*         | 84.088\*\*          | \-                   |   | 531.763\*\*         | 2650.340\*\*        |
+--------------------------------------------+---------------------+-------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+---+---------------------+---------------------+
| > Log likelihood                           | -9340               |       | -8288               | -976                | \-                   |   | 263                 | 4568                |
+--------------------------------------------+---------------------+-------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+---+---------------------+---------------------+
| > No. of parameters                        | 33                  |       | 33                  | 33                  | \-                   |   | 33                  | 33                  |
+--------------------------------------------+---------------------+-------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+---+---------------------+---------------------+
| > Meta frontier LR test                    | \-                  |       | \-                  | \-                  | \-                   |   | 676.936             | 9288.023            |
+--------------------------------------------+---------------------+-------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+---+---------------------+---------------------+
| > Ratio variance due to inefficiency       | 0.746\*\*\* (0.055) |       | 0.741\*\*\* (0.056) | 0.788\*\*\* (0.070) | \-                   |   | 0.895\*\*\* (0.146) | 0.677\*\*\* (0.250) |
+--------------------------------------------+---------------------+-------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+---+---------------------+---------------------+

Significance levels: \* p\<0.10, \*\* p\<0.05, \*\*\*p\<0.01

^a^ Null hypothesis of no one-sided error (i.e., no inefficiency) was tested.

Meta Stochastic Frontier Analysis was jointly performed on Ghana Living Standards Survey \[waves 4-7\]).

Standard errors were estimated via the jackknife resampling method by iteratively generating 100 resampled datasets by randomly excluding one enumeration area from each survey for every resample.

#### Table 4. Technology level and technical efficiency gaps based on extraction activity in Ghana

+--------------------------------------------------------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+
|                                                        | **No extraction**   | **Some extraction** | **Difference**       |
|                                                        |                     |                     |                      |
|                                                        | **\[A\]**           | **\[B\]**           | **\[B-A\]**          |
+========================================================+=====================+=====================+======================+
| [Technology gap ratio (TGR)]{.underline}               |                     |                     |                      |
+--------------------------------------------------------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+
| Any type of resource extraction                        | 0.819\*\*\* (0.011) | 0.726\*\*\* (0.019) | -0.093\*\*\* (0.009) |
+--------------------------------------------------------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+
| Any scale of mineral mining                            | 0.868\*\*\* (0.006) | 0.666\*\*\* (0.020) | -0.202\*\*\* (0.019) |
+--------------------------------------------------------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+

+--------------------------------------------------------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+
| Quarrying                                              | 0.905\*\*\* (0.003) | 0.780\*\*\* (0.017) | -0.124\*\*\* (0.015) |
+--------------------------------------------------------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+
| Sand winning                                           | 0.923\*\*\* (0.004) | 0.762\*\*\* (0.013) | -0.161\*\*\* (0.012) |
+--------------------------------------------------------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+
| [Pure farmer technical efficiency (TE)]{.underline}    |                     |                     |                      |
+--------------------------------------------------------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+
| Any type of resource extraction                        | 0.454\*\*\* (0.001) | 0.511\*\*\* (0.003) | 0.057\*\*\* (0.003)  |
+--------------------------------------------------------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+
| Any scale of mineral mining                            | 0.462\*\*\* (0.004) | 0.472\*\*\* (0.017) | 0.010 (0.018)        |
+--------------------------------------------------------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+
| Informal or small-scale mineral mining                 | 0.506\*\*\* (0.001) | 0.443\*\*\* (0.001) | -0.063\*\*\* (0.001) |
+--------------------------------------------------------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+
| Quarrying                                              | 0.486\*\*\* (0.002) | 0.515\*\*\* (0.017) | 0.029 (0.017)        |
+--------------------------------------------------------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+
| Sand winning                                           | 0.471\*\*\* (0.001) | 0.725\*\*\* (0.016) | 0.253\*\*\* (0.016)  |
+--------------------------------------------------------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+
| [Meta-frontier technical efficiency (MTE)]{.underline} |                     |                     |                      |
+--------------------------------------------------------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+
| Any type of resource extraction                        | 0.371\*\*\* (0.005) | 0.349\*\*\* (0.010) | -0.022\*\*\* (0.006) |
+--------------------------------------------------------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+
| Any scale of mineral mining                            | 0.405\*\*\* (0.004) | 0.304\*\*\* (0.005) | -0.101\*\*\* (0.004) |
+--------------------------------------------------------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+
| Informal or small-scale mineral mining                 | 0.458\*\*\* (0.002) | 0.362\*\*\* (0.002) | -0.096\*\*\* (0.001) |
+--------------------------------------------------------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+
| Quarrying                                              | 0.438\*\*\* (0.002) | 0.377\*\*\* (0.003) | -0.061\*\*\* (0.003) |
+--------------------------------------------------------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+
| Sand winning                                           | 0.434\*\*\* (0.002) | 0.523\*\*\* (0.004) | 0.089\*\*\* (0.004)  |
+--------------------------------------------------------+---------------------+---------------------+----------------------+

Significance levels: \* p\<0.10, \*\* p\<0.05, \*\*\*p\<0.01

Meta Stochastic Frontier Analysis was jointly performed on Ghana Living Standards Survey \[waves 4 to 7\]).

Standard errors were estimated via the jackknife resampling method by iteratively generating 100 resampled datasets by randomly excluding one enumeration area from each survey for every resample.

AI-generated content may be incorrect.](media/image1.png){width="6.000012029746282in" height="6.000012029746282in"}

AI-generated content may be incorrect.](media/image2.png){width="8.000016404199474in" height="5.000009842519685in"}

**Figure 3. Crop production technology adoption and technical efficiency gaps associated with natural resource extraction by major crops and administrative regions in Ghana**

![The image presents a bar chart comparing the technology gap ratio and technical efficiency across various administrative regions and major crops. AI-generated content may be incorrect.](media/image3.png){width="8.000016404199474in" height="5.000009842519685in"}

**Appendix**

**Note A1 Drivers of technical inefficiency**

Table S8 presents the key characteristics influencing whether farm households move closer or farther from their technically efficient production frontiers. Our analysis shows that a complex interaction of underlying factors determines whether a farm household operates near or farther from its efficient production frontier. Demographic factors including age, education, and gender explain technical inefficiency among Ghanaian farm households \[57\], \[73\]; however, their contributions to technical inefficiency are not uniform across several frontiers. For instance, age drives farm households away from their meta-frontier for the matched sample yet it drives them towards their group frontier. Location, crop diversification, access to credit and extension are also underlying drivers of technical inefficiency.

Focusing on the group frontier, we find that age tends to exert a uniform influence on the two farm groups (farmers in extraction areas and those in non-extraction areas). However, the influence of credit access depends on the farm group: farmers with credit access in non-extraction areas tend to move away from their efficient frontiers, while their counterparts with credit access in extraction areas tend to move closer to their efficient production frontier. Using the coastal savanna ecological zone as our reference category, we find that location matters greatly in explaining technical inefficiency for farmers in extraction areas although it (location) has no significant association with technical inefficiency for farmers in non-extraction areas. For example, Ghanaian farmers in forest and transitional ecological zones with extractive activities tend to move away from their respective technically efficient frontiers compared to their counterparts in the coastal savanna zone. Farmers in guinea savanna zones with extractive activities tend to move towards their technically efficient frontiers compared to their coastal savanna counterparts.

The mean mid-rate interbank FX rate between the Ghana cedi (GH₵) and the US Dollar (\$) for December 2019 was 5.54 GH₵/\$ as reported by the Bank of Ghana

Data Sources: Ghana Living Standards Survey \[waves 4-7\]

**Table A3. Meta stochastic frontier analysis results for Ghanaian crop producers for 1998/99**

  --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                                      **Naïve national\      ** **   **Group frontier**     ** **                  **Meta-frontier**                          
                                                      frontier**                                                                                                              
  --------------------------------------------------- ---------------------- ------- ---------------------- ---------------------- ------------------- ---------------------- ----------------------
                                                                                     **No extraction**      **Some extraction**                        **Matched**            **Unmatched**

  **[Production function]{.underline}**                                                                                                                                       

  Land \[lnI1\]                                       0.693\*\*\* (0.004)            0.714\*\*\* (0.004)    0.697\*\*\* (0.089)                        0.636\*\*\* (0.019)    0.709\*\*\* (0.004)

  Planting material \[lnI2\]                          0.051\*\*\* (0.001)            0.051\*\*\* (0.001)    0.008\* (0.004)                            0.046\*\*\* (0.005)    0.044\*\*\* (0.001)

  Family labor \[lnI3\]                               0.167\*\*\* (0.005)            0.133\*\*\* (0.004)    0.125\*\*\* (0.014)                        0.126\*\*\* (0.013)    0.156\*\*\* (0.004)

  Hired labor \[lnI4\]                                0.028\*\*\* (0.001)            0.029\*\*\* (0.001)    0.009\*\*\* (0.001)                        0.020\*\*\* (0.002)    0.029\*\*\* (0.001)

  Fertilizer \[lnI6\]                                 0.050\*\*\* (0.001)            0.050\*\*\* (0.001)    0.045\*\*\* (0.003)                        0.044\*\*\* (0.003)    0.052\*\*\* (0.001)

Significance levels: \* p\<0.10, \*\* p\<0.05, \*\*\*p\<0.01

Meta Stochastic Frontier Analysis was jointly performed on Ghana Living Standards Survey \[waves 6 and 7\]).

Standard errors were estimated via the jackknife resampling method by iteratively generating 100 resampled datasets by randomly excluding one enumeration area from each survey for every resample.

Meta Stochastic Frontier Analysis was jointly performed on Ghana Living Standards Survey \[waves 6 and 7\]).

Standard errors were estimated via the jackknife resampling method by iteratively generating 100 resampled datasets by randomly excluding one enumeration area from each survey for every resample.

**Table A6. Meta stochastic frontier analysis results for Ghanaian crop producers for 2016/17**

Meta Stochastic Frontier Analysis was jointly performed on Ghana Living Standards Survey \[waves 6 and 7\]).

Standard errors were estimated via the jackknife resampling method by iteratively generating 100 resampled datasets by randomly excluding one enumeration area from each survey for every resample.

Meta Stochastic Frontier Analysis was jointly performed on Ghana Living Standards Survey \[waves 6 and 7\]).

Standard errors were estimated via the jackknife resampling method by iteratively generating 100 resampled datasets by randomly excluding one enumeration area from each survey for every resample.

**Table A8. Determinants of crop production technical inefficiency and natural resource extraction driven technology gaps in Ghana**

  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                               **Naïve national\      ** **   **Group frontier**     ** **                 **Meta-frontier**                     
                                               frontier**                                                                                                        
  -------------------------------------------- ---------------------- ------- ---------------------- --------------------- ------------------- ----------------- ------------------
                                                                              **No extraction**      **Some extraction**                       **Matched**       **Unmatched**

  Female farmer (dummy)                        0.303\*\*\* (0.032)            0.327\*\*\* (0.043)    0.112 (0.122)                             0.208 (0.689)     0.482 (0.414)

  Age (years)                                  0.062 (0.039)                  0.091 (0.064)          -0.191 (0.369)                            -0.112 (0.259)    0.060 (0.481)

  Education (years)                            0.005 (0.006)                  0.008 (0.006)          -0.016 (0.015)                            0.016 (0.016)     0.030 (0.033)

  Land owned (dummy)                           -0.153 (0.109)                 -0.184\* (0.095)       0.103 (0.370)                             0.142 (0.159)     0.063 (0.557)

  Crop diversification (index)                 0.215 (0.314)                  0.232 (0.351)          -0.045 (0.527)                            0.447 (0.743)     0.614 (0.440)

  Mechanization (dummy)                        -0.028 (0.268)                 -0.106 (0.257)         8.903 (15.636)                            0.269 (7.544)     -0.267 (1.061)

  Credit (dummy)                               0.126\*\*\* (0.045)            0.140\*\* (0.068)      0.106 (0.193)                             0.015 (3.711)     -0.206 (0.233)

  Extension (dummy)                            -0.030 (0.126)                 -0.014 (0.134)         -0.020 (0.514)                            0.036 (7.905)     -0.094 (2.396)

  Ecological zone \[base = Coastal Savanna\]                                                                                                                     

  Forest                                       0.405\*\* (0.201)              0.413\*\* (0.201)      8.100 (14.717)                            -0.723 (13.469)   0.631 (2.735)

  Guinea Savanah                               -1.253\*\*\* (0.440)           -1.202\*\*\* (0.423)   -2.689 (2.393)                            -9.268 (14.061)   -10.012 (16.722)

  Sudan Savanah                                -3.859 (3.414)                 -3.710 (2.573)         -3.305 (29.159)                           -1.333 (19.776)   -3.172 (5.004)

  Intercept                                    0.193 (0.291)                  0.062 (0.283)          -6.721 (15.334)                           -1.319 (18.487)   -4.856 (3.724)
  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Significance levels: \* p\<0.10, \*\* p\<0.05, \*\*\*p\<0.01

^a^ Null hypothesis of no one-sided error (i.e., no inefficiency) was tested.

Meta Stochastic Frontier Analysis was jointly performed on Ghana Living Standards Survey \[waves 6 and 7\]).

Standard errors were estimated via the jackknife resampling method by iteratively generating 100 resampled datasets by randomly excluding one enumeration area from each survey for every resample.

**Figure A1. Covariate balancing summary**

![The image is a scatter plot showing various factors (e.g., household characteristics, crop diversification, demographic and socio-economic variables) against their Absolute Standardized Mean Differences, Variance Ratios, and Kolmogorov-Smirnov statistics. AI-generated content may be incorrect.](media/image4.png){width="9.0in" height="5.727083333333334in"}

**Figure A2. Different model specification generally shows decreasing impact of natural resource extraction on crop production output for Ghanaian farmers (2012-2017)**

![The diagram illustrates various statistical models and distributions used to evaluate technical efficiency in production, including the meta-frontier, different types of production functions, and matched sample techniques. AI-generated content may be incorrect.](media/image5.png){width="6.5in" height="7.455555555555556in"}

Meta Stochastic Frontier Analysis was jointly performed on Ghana Living Standards Survey \[waves 6 and 7\]).

Standard errors were estimated via the jackknife resampling method by iteratively generating 100 resampled datasets by randomly excluding one enumeration area from each survey for every resample.

[^1]: Countries like Nigeria, Angola, and Ghana heavily rely on oil and gas \[10\], \[11\], \[12\], \[13\], while others such as the Democratic Republic of Congo and South Africa depend on mining industries \[14\].

[^2]: The listed activities in the community questionnaire to be ranked included farming, fishing, trading, handicraft, salt mining, gold mining (commercial), gold mining (galamsey), diamond, bauxite, sand winning, quarrying.

[^3]: <https://mofa.gov.gh/site/images/pdf/Feed_Ghana_Programme.pdf>

[^4]: <https://goldbod.gov.gh/press-release/press-statement14th-april-2025/>

[^5]: First, we estimate the unrestricted production frontier model. Second, we use the estimated parameters and covariance matrix from step one to derive restricted parameters through quadratic optimization. This ensures positive marginal products for every observation. Third, we estimate efficiency and examine its determinants using the theoretically consistent frontier obtained in step two. The maximum achievable output, derived from the restricted parameters, serves as the dependent variable. Inefficiency variables are then reintroduced into the model, consistent with step one.
